title: What Became of the Chimps Who Revolutionized Our Understanding of Animal Behavior?
author: Devin Reese
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementPsychologyWhat Became of the Chimps Who Revolutionized Our Understanding of Animal Behavior?It’s a harrowing tale of deadly winters in Berlin captivityDRBy Devin Reese11:00 AM CDT on July 17, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyIf it weren’t for Sultan and his primate clan, the study of animal behavior would have taken a very different path. Sultan, a male chimpanzee (Pan troglodytes), and five wild females were captured as juveniles in South Cameroon and made captive in 1912. They were housed on Tenerife, a Canary Island off North Africa, until 1920, during which several chimpanzees were added. Research on these chimps, led by German psychologist Wolfgang Köhler, laid the groundwork for comparing how animals behave, or the field of “comparative psychology.”Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Shielding Earth With The Help of This Moon-Dust SunscreenShareNextStayA recent paper published in the journal European Psychologist tracked the fate of Köhler’s chimpanzee study subjects after they were moved from Tenerife to a zoo in Berlin. Led by University of Auckland psychologist Javier Virués-Ortega, the paper represents the culmination of archival-style detective work—analyzing newspapers, photos, zoo records, letters, memoirs, museum specimens, public records, and interviews—to piece together the final years of the famous Tenerife chimps.“These chimpanzees are usually remembered for what they revealed about the mind,” said Virués-Ortega in a press release. “We wanted to ask the historical question textbooks mostly leave out: What happened to the animals themselves?”Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisement
